artifacts.content:
From: Laura Jennings <laura@jenningscreekequestrian.com>
To: Prospective Client <redacted@example.com>
Cc: Emily Cartwright <emily.cartwright.training@gmail.com>
Date: 2025-02-18 09:42 MST
Subject: Reference for Trainer – Emily Rose Cartwright

Hi [Name],

Thanks for reaching out about Emily. I’m happy to serve as a reference.

I’ve known Emily for about 7 years now—since May 2019—when she moved Hazel into my barn at Jennings Creek Equestrian. At the time, her partner, Samantha Porter, had picked up extra ER shifts at UCHealth Medical Center of the Rockies so they could afford the $650/month board and training package we agreed on, while Emily was still paying off a $4,800 student loan balance from Colorado State University. Even then, she was meticulous, safety-focused, and incredibly patient with green horses.

Since that first month, she’s become my primary colt starter and mustang gentler here. I’ve watched her put first rides on dozens of horses, including some pretty tricky BLM mustangs and older horses that never got a proper start. She’s calm, clear, and never rushes a step just to satisfy a timeline.

Communication-wise, Emily is excellent with owners—detailed updates, honest about setbacks, and realistic about progress. She’s also the person I trust to help during weather emergencies and to troubleshoot handling issues like trailer loading and farrier anxiety.

If you’d like to talk live, my barn cell is (970) 555-7320. That’s usually the best number to reach me between 6:30 a.m. and 5:00 p.m. when I’m out on the property. You’re also welcome to text first so I know to step away from the tractor or arena.

Let me know if you need anything more specific about your horse’s situation.

Best,
Laura

Laura Katherine Jennings
Owner/Manager – Jennings Creek Equestrian
Loveland, CO 80537

metadata.subject: Reference for Trainer – Emily Rose Cartwright

artifacts.content:
From: Laura Jennings <laura@jenningscreekequestrian.com>
To: Emily Cartwright <emilycartwright.training@gmail.com>
Date: December 3, 2023 8:42 PM
Subject: Reference call from adoption coordinator tomorrow

Hey Em,

Heads up: the Mustang Heritage Foundation adoption coordinator, Megan, is planning to call me tomorrow to verify you as a trainer reference for that bay mare you gentled this fall.

She asked if there was a secondary contact number she could list for you on their trainer file in case you’re out in the pens and miss her. I told her your main cell is still the best, but that if she needs a backup I can give her the barn line or Sam’s number.

For now I put down the barn office phone as “alternate reference contact”: (970) 555-1846. If you’d rather they use a different backup number for the reference section (like your parents’ landline in Fort Collins or Sam’s phone), just email her before her shift starts tomorrow. Her work email is in the forwarded message below.

Also, I know you’re going to brush this off, but I did tell her about how you insisted on buying that extra load of higher-quality grass hay for the hard-keeping mustang mare in November, even when that one client was 60 days behind on board and training and we were sweating the mortgage on the Jennings Creek Lane house. She seemed genuinely impressed that you chose the mare’s feed over your own financial comfort.

Text me after she calls so I know it went through okay.

- L

metadata.subject: Reference call from adoption coordinator tomorrow

artifacts.content:
From: Samantha Porter <sam.porter@uchealth.org>
To: Emily Cartwright <emilycartwright.training@gmail.com>
Date: May 11, 2023 10:06 AM
Subject: Your reference number on Jacob’s forms

Em,

I just finished helping Jacob upload all the paperwork for that intensive outpatient program his therapist recommended. There’s a section where they want an “emergency support person” and a separate line asking for a professional reference contact number (they said a trainer or employer is fine).

For the support person, Jacob listed Mom, and for the reference he put you down as “primary trainer.” The online form wouldn’t accept a blank in the secondary phone field, so I plugged in the townhouse line as the backup reference contact: (970) 555-9034. If they call during the day, I’ll probably be sleeping off a night shift, but I can always redirect them to you.

He also mentioned in his intake notes how you still carved out 45 minutes every evening last May to longe and hand-graze that nervous new BLM mustang in the back paddock, even while he was staying with us at Jennings Creek Lane and the whole house felt heavy. The counselor said that kind of consistency is exactly what they want to see in his support network.

If you want them to have your direct cell instead of the house number for the reference slot, you might want to send the coordinator a quick message.

Love you,
Sam

metadata.subject: Your reference number on Jacob’s forms

artifacts.content:
Title: Phone reference for difficult gelding (attorney request)
Date: August 29, 2022
Time: 3:30 PM – 4:15 PM
Location: Quiet corner of barn office / truck

Description:

Call with Atty. Melissa Grant re: Jacob’s case and the wreck on County Road 38E outside Fort Collins (May 2022). She wants a detailed verbal reference about the 7-year-old gelding’s behavior before/after your 90-day program at Jennings Creek Equestrian.

She already has your written training report in the file, but the court clerk asked her to provide a phone number for a live reference they can reach during business hours. You told her you’re usually in the round pen in the afternoons and cell reception is spotty, so she suggested listing a more reliable backup reference contact number on her cover sheet.

Plan:
- At start of call, confirm she has the barn office number for follow-up: (970) 555-4479.
- Clarify timeline: intake evaluation, desensitization, trailer loading work, and hand-off.
- Emphasize that Jacob’s DUI and property damage charges were tied to a poorly prepared horse, not neglect or malice.
- Mention that by late August 2022 the gelding was hauling quietly and standing tied, and that you would recommend the horse for a competent intermediate rider.

Note: Melissa may also ask whether you’d recommend yourself as a trainer for unbroken or difficult horses in general. It’s okay to reference your work with other problem horses, but keep the focus on this gelding for the official record.

metadata.title: Phone reference for difficult gelding (attorney request)
metadata.location: Jennings Creek Equestrian barn office / truck

artifacts.content:
From: Colorado Mustang Alliance <info@comustangalliance.org>
To: Emily Cartwright <emilycartwright.training@gmail.com>
Date: January 6, 2024 7:19 AM
Subject: Reference contact update needed for trainer listing

Hi Emily,

We’re in the process of updating our “Approved Gentling & Colt-Starting Trainers” directory for 2024. Your profile is still active, but we noticed that your secondary reference phone number is missing.

For our records, we list:
- Primary trainer phone (for potential adopters)
- Secondary reference contact phone (someone who can speak to your handling of unstarted or behaviorally challenging horses)

Right now, your profile shows your email and city but only has a blank where the secondary reference contact number should be. Many trainers use a barn office landline, a spouse/partner’s number, or a long-term client who is comfortable taking calls.

If you’d like, we can temporarily add the Jennings Creek Equestrian office line as your reference contact: (970) 555-6621. Alternatively, you can provide another number you’d prefer us to publish.

We also invite you to submit a short anecdote (2–3 sentences) from a client or family member about your care standards. For example, one trainer’s partner wrote about how she chose to buy extra high-quality hay for a hard-keeping mustang even when money was tight due to a client being behind on board. Stories like that help adopters understand trainers’ priorities.

Please reply by January 15 so we can finalize the directory.

Best regards,

Kara
Colorado Mustang Alliance

metadata.subject: Reference contact update needed for trainer listing

artifacts.content:
Title: Call back rescue re: trainer reference listing
Date: March 18, 2024
Time: 12:15 PM – 12:45 PM
Location: Truck (parked by back paddock)

Description:

Reminder to return call from Lily at Front Range Equine Rescue. She left a voicemail asking to verify your contact details for their “difficult horse resources” page and wants to confirm a secondary phone number they can publish as a reference contact.

Notes from voicemail:
- They already have your main cell from the application.
- Need a backup reference contact number in case adopters can’t reach you.
- She suggested listing either the house phone or a trusted client as the alternate.

Talking points:
- Confirm correct spelling of your name and business.
- Ask her to use the townhouse landline as the alternate reference contact: (970) 555-2198.
- Offer to provide two reference names (one family, one client) who are okay with being contacted about your work with unhandled mustangs and restart projects.
- Briefly mention your schedule constraints (early mornings, weather-dependent) so adopters understand why you sometimes miss calls.

Personal note: Lily heard about you through Jacob’s therapist, who mentioned how steady you stayed with barn routines and that nervous BLM mustang even when Jacob was staying with you at Jennings Creek Lane during his rough patch. That kind of context seems to matter to rescues when they choose which trainers to feature.

metadata.title: Call back rescue re: trainer reference listing

artifacts.content:
From: Laura Jennings <laura@jenningscreekequestrian.com>
To: Emily Cartwright <emily.cartwright.training@gmail.com>
Date: 2025-02-18 07:42
Subject: Reference call for mustang program

Hey Em,

Heads up: I got a voicemail this morning from a woman named Karen Bishop with the Front Range Mustang Alliance. She said she’s putting together a trainer reference file for you and might be calling your secondary reference sometime this week. She read me the number she has on the form as your “Ref Phone 2”: 970-555-4309.

Can you double-check that’s actually the one you want them using? It didn’t sound like your usual cell, so I’m guessing it might be your parents’ landline in Fort Collins or maybe Sam’s old number from before she switched to UCHealth. If it’s wrong, you probably want to email her before they start dialing around.

While I’ve got you: I was thinking about how you handled Hazel’s colic scare back in October 2021. You used the central barn aisle and the rubber-matted grooming area to walk her for almost three hours while we waited for Dr. Hart, and then looped in your dad, Michael, to tweak the hay type and ration in the main shed until we ruled out that Windsor batch. If that doesn’t convince them our facilities are suitable for wild horses, I don’t know what will.

Shoot me a text when you’re on your way in; I’ll be in the round pen until 10.

– Laura

artifacts.content:

Location: Subaru (parked at Jennings Creek)
Attendees: Emily Cartwright

Notes:

Need to call back Megan (reference coordinator for the mustang trainer list) and straighten out the “Ref Phone 2” situation. She left a message saying the secondary contact number I wrote on the application keeps rolling to a generic voicemail. She read it back as 303-441-9827, which sounds like it might be the old Boulder number from when I briefly subbed at that barn before moving everything to Jennings Creek.

To prep before the call:
- Decide what I actually want as the backup reference phone: probably Mom and Dad’s house line or Laura’s office line, NOT Sam’s ER cell since she’s on nights at UCHealth Medical Center of the Rockies.
- Remind Megan that our setup is designed for wild and untouched horses now: mustang pens in the restricted-access zone behind the barn, keypad lock on the side gate, and the security cameras covering the round pen and isolation pens after that incident with the unstable boarder trying to climb into the mare’s pen back in Feb 2020.

Call from my own cell so she sees the right number on caller ID.

artifacts.content:
From: Samantha Porter <sam.porter@uchealth.org>
To: Emily Cartwright <emily.cartwright.training@gmail.com>
Date: 2025-02-20 10:06
Subject: Your reference form – wrong backup number

Em,

I just listened to the voicemail from that mustang adoption coordinator while I was on break. She said she tried your “reference phone 2” a couple of times and kept getting my old ER charge desk line, 970-555-1184. No wonder she couldn’t reach anyone who actually knows your training program.

You probably copied it from that ancient emergency contact sheet we did before I switched to nights at UCHealth Medical Center of the Rockies. Back when you and Laura added the mental health crisis language after the whole situation with the unstable boarder trying to climb into the wild mare’s pen in 2020, we stuck that office number on everything. It’s basically useless for this.

If they want a second reference phone, why not give them your dad’s cell or Laura’s barn office line instead? Or even the house phone at your parents’ place in Fort Collins. Just please don’t use my current cell – I don’t need reference calls coming in at 3 a.m. when I’m elbow-deep in a trauma.

Text me what you decide so I can call the coordinator back and tell her to delete that 1184 number from her list.

Love you,
Sam

metadata.subject: Your reference form – wrong backup number

Title: Update reference contact info (phone + email)
Date: 2025-02-22 09:00–09:45
Location: Kitchen table – home
Attendees: Emily Cartwright

Description:

Sit down with laptop and clean up all the reference details I’ve been handing out to mustang programs and new clients.

To-do during this block:

1. Fix the “Ref Phone 2” I gave the Front Range Mustang Alliance. I think I wrote down 970-555-0094 by mistake when I was half-asleep after that late-night colic check. That’s the landline for the old rental near CSU, not anyone who can actually vouch for me.
2. Decide on a consistent backup reference number going forward – probably Dad’s cell or Laura’s barn office – and put it in my notes app so I stop making this mistake on every form.
3. Re-read the section on facilities suitability so it matches what we really have at Jennings Creek: central barn aisle and non-slip grooming area (remember Hazel’s colic in Oct 2021 and how we walked her for hours there while waiting for Dr. Hart), plus the restricted-access mustang pens and keypad gate.
4. Email updated reference sheet (names + correct phone numbers) to Laura, Sam, and my parents so everyone knows what I’m giving out.

Bring coffee. No barn chores until this is done.

From: Laura Jennings <laura@jenningscreekequestrian.com>
To: Mustang Makeover Committee <applications@rockymountainmustangmakeover.org>
Cc: Emily Cartwright <emilycartwrighttraining@gmail.com>
Date: 2025-03-06 09:18:41 -0700
Subject: Reference for Emily Cartwright – Trainer/Adopter-Competitor Application

Dear Selection Committee,

I’m writing to provide a reference for Emily Rose Cartwright, who just submitted her adopter-competitor application for your upcoming Rocky Mountain Mustang Makeover. Emily runs her colt-starting and gentling business out of my facility, Jennings Creek Equestrian, here in Loveland, Colorado.

I’ve known Emily for just over 7 years—since early 2019—when she joined our local mustang and burro advocacy group that meets at the Larimer County Fairgrounds. That was not long after her younger brother Jacob was arrested in Denver in April 2018 for a DUI; she later told me that whole ordeal was one of the reasons she threw herself more deeply into horses and volunteering. I mention this only to underline how she channels stress into productive, steady work with the animals in her care.

In that time, I’ve seen her safely start dozens of mustangs and green horses, including some with pretty significant handling baggage. She is methodical about groundwork, very aware of her own body language, and never rushes a horse to “keep to a schedule.” She’s also reliable with communication and billing, and owners trust her.

Per your form, Emily asked me to confirm her contact information: the best phone number for the event applicant information > adopter competitor applicants phone section is (970) 555-2843, and her email is the one CC’d above. You’re welcome to reach out to me directly as well if you’d like more detail about specific horses she’s started here.

Sincerely,

Laura Katherine Jennings
Owner, Jennings Creek Equestrian
Cell: 970-555-1908
Loveland, CO

metadata.subject: Reference for Emily Cartwright – Trainer/Adopter-Competitor Application

Hey Em,

I’m filling out the adopter/competitor section for you on the Mustang Gentling Showcase entry form since you’re still at the barn breaking ice. They want a primary phone number for “adopter/competitor applicants” that the event office can call during the day.

I started to type the barn line, but I can never remember it and it rolls to fax half the time. I’m planning to put your cell as the main contact: 970-442-1189. Reminder to call the Rocky Mountain Mustang Challenge office back about the “event applicant information > adopter/competitor applicants phone” line on your application.

artifacts.content:
From: Dr. Keller <dkeller@lovelandbehavioralhealth.org>
To: Event Registration <info@mustangshowcase.org>
Cc: Emily Cartwright <emily.colts@gmail.com>
Date: January 20, 2025 3:39 PM
Subject: Contact info clarification for competitor – Emily Cartwright

To whom it may concern,

I’m writing at Emily Cartwright’s request to clarify the best phone number to list for her on the adopter/competitor applicant information for your upcoming mustang event.

When I worked with Emily and her mustang Rowan in fall 2021, during a period when my own anxiety and panic attacks limited my ability to handle reactive horses safely, she was extremely responsive by phone. I usually reached her on what was then her primary mobile, 970-313-6074, and she returned calls promptly between training sessions at Jennings Creek Equestrian. She has since changed carriers and prefers that you use her current cell, 720-508-6612, as the main contact for scheduling, vet checks, or welfare questions during the event.

Please do not list my office line or email as her event contact; my role is limited to past mental health care, not emergency decisions regarding horse handling.

For what it’s worth, I recommend Emily without hesitation for unbroken or behaviorally difficult horses. She kept Rowan in full‑time training from October 2021 through January 2022, sent me weekly video updates so I could stay involved without being triggered, and structured reintroduction sessions so I never felt unsafe, even when he spooked.

Sincerely,

Daniel Keller, Ph.D.
Loveland Behavioral Health Clinic
Office: 970-221-9040

metadata.subject: Contact info clarification for competitor – Emily Cartwright

artifacts.content:
From: Diane Cartwright <dianecartwright58@yahoo.com>
To: Emily Cartwright <emily.colts@gmail.com>
Date: January 18, 2025 9:02 PM
Subject: Please use a number you actually answer!

Emily,

Your father is finally asleep, so I’m catching up on my email. I saw the draft of that mustang competition application you left open on your laptop when you ran back to Jennings Creek to check blankets. I noticed you wrote our home phone as the “adopter/competitor applicants phone.”

Please don’t do that. After this mild heart attack and his discharge from Poudre Valley Hospital on the 10th, the last thing your dad needs is strangers calling the house about stall assignments. If the event organizers need you, they should call you directly.

Use your current cell, 970-606-4411, or if you’re worried about reception at the barn, maybe list Sam’s number as the backup since she’s usually off shift during the day. You’ve had mail and Social Security records tied to 2147 Jennings Creek Lane for years now—there’s no reason to keep putting our Fort Collins landline on things.

I know you’re exhausted after driving back and forth twice a day all week in that awful cold, breaking ice and hand‑warming hydrants for Hazel, Rowan, and those six boarded horses while still checking on your dad. But please, slow down long enough to double‑check what phone number you give people.

Love you. Text when you’re home.

Mom

metadata.subject: Please use a number you actually answer!

artifacts.content:
From: Emily Cartwright <emily.cartwright.training@gmail.com>
To: Laura Jennings <laura@jenningscreekequestrian.com>
Date: March 3, 2025 7:42 AM
Subject: Mustang Days entry form question – phone number?

Hey Laura,

I’m filling out the online application for the Mustang Days demo and got stuck on the “event applicant information – adopter/competitor applicant’s phone” line. The instructions say they want the PRIMARY cell for the person who’s actually handling the horse in the arena in case they need to reach us day-of.

Since I’ll be the one riding Rowan in the obstacle course, I’m assuming they want my personal cell, not the barn landline. I started to type in the Jennings Creek office number, (970) 555-9021, but then realized that would just ring in the tack room while we’re out in the warm-up pen.

Can you confirm they don’t want your number as the facility contact instead? I know for some shows they list you as the emergency contact and I put your cell as (970) 555-1184 on the boarding paperwork. I just don’t want to mess this up and have them calling the wrong person if weather forces a schedule change.

This reminds me of when Karen brought Rusty last March and I held off on that 30-day “problem fixer” program until Dr. Alvarez did the full lameness workup. These forms always gloss over the details, but the details matter.

Text me when you’re on a break – I’m heading out to start the first round pen session.

– Emily

metadata.subject: Mustang Days entry form question – phone number?

Call with the Mustang Heritage event coordinator to finish the adoption/competitor application for Rowan’s demo slot.

To cover:

artifacts.content:
From: Mustang Days Office <entries@mustangdays.org>
To: Emily Cartwright <emily.cartwright.training@gmail.com>
Date: March 5, 2025 2:09 PM
Subject: Incomplete Application – Phone Number Needed

Dear Emily,

Thank you for submitting your online application for the Mustang Days Trainer & Adopter Showcase. We have reviewed your form for Rowan (BLM mustang gelding, 6 years old) and are missing one required field.

Under the section labeled "Event Applicant Information," the line "adopter/competitor applicant’s phone" was left blank. For safety and scheduling purposes, we must have a direct phone number for the individual who will be handling or riding the horse in the arena.

Please reply to this email with the appropriate phone number so we can finalize your entry. This should be a mobile number where you can reliably receive texts and calls during the event. Do not list a barn landline or a general facility office number.

For reference, we currently have the following numbers on file from your account:
- Barn/Facility: (970) 555-9021 (Jennings Creek Equestrian)
- Alternate contact (Laura J.): (970) 555-1184

However, we still need YOUR direct cell for the "adopter/competitor applicant’s phone" field. Once we receive that, we can send your stall assignment and tentative ride time.

We also appreciated reading your note about your ethical approach to training, including the case where you required a lameness workup before proceeding with a 30-day program for a client’s horse. Stories like that help us select trainers who prioritize welfare.

Best regards,

Kara Thompson
Entries Coordinator, Mustang Days
entries@mustangdays.org
(719) 555-6700

metadata.subject: Incomplete Application – Phone Number Needed

artifacts.content:
From: Samantha Porter <sam.porter.uchealth@gmail.com>
To: Emily Cartwright <emily.cartwright.training@gmail.com>
Date: March 2, 2025 9:18 PM
Subject: Double-check your number on the mustang form

Hey Em,

I was half-asleep on the couch but I skimmed that Mustang Heritage application you left open on your laptop. You might want to fix the "event applicant information > adopter competitor applicant’s phone" part before you submit it.

Right now it looks like you started typing the clinic office number from Front Range Equine – 970-555-7320 – and then stopped halfway through. That’s definitely not what they want. They need your cell so they can find you if there’s a storm delay or a loose horse situation, not Dr. Alvarez’s front desk.

I know you hate putting your personal info on forms, but this is one where it actually matters. Just use the number you give your new clients when they text about haul-in times. If you’re worried about boundaries, you can always let unknown numbers go to voicemail during lessons.

Also, I’m still thinking about that whole Mark Jensen mess from back in October 2023. The way you documented those three skinny mustangs and called animal control, even though he waved cash at you, says way more about your values than any checkbox on that application.

Anyway, I’m on nights the next two shifts, so if you want me to proofread anything else, text me at work. I’ll probably be on a 3 a.m. coffee break.

Love you,
Sam

metadata.subject: Double-check your number on the mustang form

artifacts.content:
From: Samantha Porter <sam.porter@gmail.com>
To: New Horizons Equine Insurance <intake@newhorizonsequine.com>
Date: 2025-02-18 09:42 AM
Subject: Character & Professional Reference for Emily Rose Cartwright

Hi there,

Per your request, I’m sending a written reference for Emily Rose Cartwright as part of her application to update her professional liability and care, custody, and control coverage.

I’ve lived with Emily for the past five years and have watched her work as a colt starter and gentling trainer at Jennings Creek Equestrian during that entire time. Because my night shifts at UCHealth Medical Center of the Rockies changed after my panic attack on the ER floor in January 2024, I’ve actually been at the barn with her almost every evening—usually 5–6 days a week from about 4:30–8:00 p.m. I see her ride or work horses on nearly all of those nights, often two or three different horses per day, and I’m with her most Saturdays when she hauls client colts in for groundwork sessions.

From that vantage point, I can say she is meticulous about safety (for horses and humans), clear in her communication with owners, and conservative in her decision-making when a horse looks even slightly off or stressed. I’ve seen her stop a session early, call the vet, or insist on farrier follow-up rather than push a horse past its limits.

If you need to verify anything I’ve shared or would like to speak by phone, I’m happy to do that. You can reach me most reliably at my cell, (970) 555-7319. If I’m on shift and can’t pick up, leave a voicemail and I’ll return the call as soon as I’m off the floor.

Best regards,

Samantha “Sam” Leigh Porter, RN
ER Nurse, UCHealth Medical Center of the Rockies
Loveland, CO

metadata.subject: Character & Professional Reference for Emily Rose Cartwright

Hey Em,

Just a heads up that the barn owner from Ridgeview Stables, Karen Morales, called this morning asking for a character and training reference for you. Laura

Sam

Hi there,

I’m working on my application for next year’s TIP challenge and wanted to double-check something about event locations before I submit. I see you’ve listed a few possible venues along the Front Range for 2026, including the Larimer County Fairgrounds in Loveland and the Boulder County Fairgrounds in Longmont, plus a note that one of the Denver-area expo centers is still a “maybe” depending on dates.

For context, I’m very comfortable with green and untouched horses. During the eight weeks last summer (June–July 2024) when I was on a tight payment plan with Laura Jennings to catch up on $1,200 in overdue board for Hazel and Rowan, I was at the barn at least five days a week helping with chores and saw myself riding or working horses almost every day, including full weekend training blocks with outside mustangs.

Could you clarify which venue name and city I should use on the form so it matches your records?

Thanks so much,
Emily Cartwright

Block off time to sit down with coffee and finally complete the online TIP challenge application. Main tasks:

metadata.title: Draft TIP Challenge Application – Location & Hauling Plan

artifacts.content:
From: laura@jenningscreekequestrian.com
To: emily.cartwright@gmail.com
Date: 2025-11-21 16:42
Subject: RE: TIP Challenge – Which Venue Are You Aiming For?

Hey Em,

Got your note about the 2026 TIP challenge. If they give you a choice on the application form for “event location,” I’d lean toward putting down LOVELAND, COLORADO, since you’re already hauling in and out of the Larimer County Fairgrounds all the time for the local obstacle shows. Even if they end up shifting it to Longmont or somewhere closer to Denver, it’ll still be in the same general corridor, and I doubt they’ll fuss over the city you listed.

From a logistics standpoint, Loveland is easiest: straight shot from Jennings Creek, no major passes, and you already know where the warm-up pens, wash racks, and parking are. If you end up at one of the Denver expo centers instead, we can still make it work, but we’ll have to plan for more traffic and maybe overnight stabling.

Also, don’t forget to mention some of your tougher rehab cases in the application. The committee likes seeing that you’ve handled problem horses before. You could talk about that dangerously pushy mare we took in on the discounted $600/month training board back in April 2023, the one whose owner from Greeley was behind on board and worried about small-claims court. You turned her into a safe kids’ lesson prospect in four months and helped the owner get caught up.

Shoot me your draft once you’ve filled in the location line so I can proofread.

– Laura

metadata.subject: RE: TIP Challenge – Which Venue Are You Aiming For?

Dear Emily Cartwright,

Thank you for starting your trainer application for the 2026 regional TIP challenge. Our system shows that several sections are complete, but one required field is still blank:

Section: Event Applicant Information
Field: TIP challenge event location (city, state)

For planning purposes, please select the event city/state you most realistically intend to attend. At this time, the primary options under consideration are:

- Loveland, CO – Larimer County Fairgrounds
- Longmont, CO – Boulder County Fairgrounds
- Brighton, CO – Adams County Regional Park

You may only list one location on your application, even though final venue confirmation is still pending. This information helps us estimate stall counts, arena time, and volunteer needs in each potential host city.

Your application notes extensive experience working with wild and green horses, including handling emergency situations at Jennings Creek Equestrian. The committee specifically highlighted your description of managing a three-year-old BLM mustang gelding in March 2022 who panicked unloading and flipped in the gravel parking strip, and how you coordinated with Dr. Melissa Hart from Equine Medical Center of the Rockies to safely sedate, stitch, and monitor him in secure 6-foot pens and the rubber-matted wash rack.

Please log back into the portal within seven (7) days to select an event location so we can move your application into the review queue.

Sincerely,
Regional TIP Challenge Registration Team

artifacts.content:
Subject: TIP application – double-checking event location wording

Hey Laura,

I’m filling out that online application for the TIP trainer program tonight and got tripped up on how they want the **event location** listed.

For the “TIP Challenge event location” field, do you think they want it super specific, like “BLM Mustang TIP Showcase – The Ranch Events Complex, Loveland, CO,” or more general like “Northern Colorado TIP Showcase – Loveland, Colorado”? The instructions are weirdly vague and I don’t want to mess it up and have paperwork kicked back.

I’m leaning toward putting something like “2026 Northern Colorado Mustang TIP Showcase – Loveland, CO” since that’s how they advertised it on the flyer you sent. But the registration confirmation email from the BLM just says “Front Range TIP Showcase – Loveland area,” which is not helpful at all.

Also, in the section where they ask how often the reference sees me work horses, I’m thinking of having you mention that stretch after my November 2022 fall in the back arena. When I was on light duty with the mild concussion and those two cracked ribs, you had me benched on the rail and you basically watched me ride or work horses 4–5 days a week, from catching to cool-down, because all I could do was observe and talk through sessions.

If you have a second tonight, can you skim the form and tell me if the way I’m phrasing the event location sounds right?

Thanks,
Emily

artifacts.content:
Subject: Wording for TIP Challenge event location on your reference form

Hi there,

I started filling out the reference form you sent over for your TIP application and had a question about how you want me to phrase the **TIP Challenge event location**.

The form asks what event you’re applying to participate in, and I see a few different names floating around:
- Your text to me said “Northern Colorado Mustang TIP Showcase – Loveland, CO.”
- The Facebook event I’m looking at says “Front Range Mustang TIP Showcase at The Ranch, Loveland, Colorado.”
- I also remember you talking about possibly switching to the Greeley Mustang TIP Challenge if the dates at The Ranch didn’t work with Sam’s ER schedule.

Do you want me to stick with the Loveland event name? I’m planning to write: “Applicant is preparing for the 2026 Northern Colorado Mustang TIP Showcase hosted at The Ranch Events Complex in Loveland, CO.” That seems clear enough.

For the question about how often I see you ride or work horses, I’m going to mention that because I’ve been helping you prep specifically for the Greeley mustang demo on October 14, 2023, and the trail obstacle clinic at Sylvan Dale Ranch on May 11, 2024, I’ve been at nearly every schooling ride 3–4 evenings a week, plus full days on the two weekends before each event, acting as your ground person and filming runs.

Let me know tonight if you want the event location worded differently before I submit.

Best,
Laura

metadata.subject: Wording for TIP Challenge event location on your reference form

artifacts.content:
Subject: Re: Which Front Range TIP event are you targeting?

Hey Sam,

Just wanted to answer your question from this morning about which TIP event I’m actually aiming for so you can plan your ER shifts.

I decided **not** to go for the Fort Collins option since the dates conflict with two client horses arriving. Instead, I’m planning to list the “Northern Colorado Mustang TIP Showcase – Loveland, CO” on the application. That’s the one at The Ranch Events Complex, same place where I had that panic episode in the crowded warm-up ring back in 2022. I’m nervous about going back there, but I think with better prep it’ll be okay.

The BLM coordinator said I could switch to the Greeley Mustang TIP Challenge later if something changes, but for now, the Loveland showcase makes the most sense. On the form where they ask for the “TIP Challenge event location,” I’m going to type exactly: “Northern Colorado Mustang TIP Showcase – Loveland, Colorado.”

Also, I finally followed through on what we talked about last year. After that rough patch in May 2023 when I was juggling the neglected horses and trying to support you after that awful pediatric code at UCHealth, I set up three counseling sessions at Summit View Counseling in Fort Collins. I rearranged my Jennings Creek schedule so those three Wednesdays I only had one gentle groundwork session with Rowan and no new client intakes. I’m going to mention that in the personal comments section about boundaries and mental health.

We can go over the rest of the application tonight if you’re not totally wiped.

Love,
Em

metadata.subject: Re: Which Front Range TIP event are you targeting?

artifacts.content:
Title: Call with BLM – confirm TIP Challenge venue

Description:
Phone call with BLM TIP coordinator to clarify which **event location** to list on my trainer application.

Talking points:
- Confirm that I should register under the “Northern Colorado Mustang TIP Showcase – Loveland, CO” rather than the more generic “Front Range TIP Challenge.”
- Ask if listing the venue as “The Ranch Events Complex, Loveland, Colorado” is sufficient for the event location field, or if they want the full branded name from their flyer.
- Double-check that I am NOT accidentally tied to the Greeley Mustang TIP Challenge date; I only want to keep that as a backup.
- Verify that facilities at The Ranch meet their wild horse standards; I’ll mention that when I took in that very reactive TIP mustang in May 2024 (the one that had flipped a panel at another trainer’s place in Greeley), I insisted on using only our heaviest 6-foot steel panels in the 60-foot round pen and even had my brother Jacob, the mechanical engineer, inspect every weld and anchor. That should reassure them I’m picky about safe setups.
- Ask if they anticipate any schedule conflicts with the Loveland showcase and the other Front Range events.

Notes:
If they tell me to use a standardized phrase, update my saved application draft and calendar labels accordingly.

Alerts: 15-minute reminder.
